questions.question: What makes threat modeling useful in a system design interview?
options: It prioritizes realistic risks and changes the design instead of listing buzzwords.
It replaces the need for architecture discussion.
It only matters for banking systems.
It guarantees every attack can be prevented.
questions.explanation: Threat modeling is useful when it sharpens the design and trade-offs.

questions.question: Why should threat models start with assets and boundaries?
options: Because risk depends on what is valuable and where trust changes.
Because attackers always target only databases.
Because observability is impossible without them.
Because quotas are a form of encryption.
questions.explanation: Assets and trust boundaries anchor which threats are worth discussing.

questions.question: What is the clearest sign that a threat model improved the design answer?
options: It led to concrete changes like stronger authz boundaries, quotas, or audit controls.
It added five more acronyms.
It removed all trade-offs from the answer.
It replaced the capacity model entirely.
questions.explanation: Useful threat modeling changes architecture and operations, not just vocabulary.

questions.question: Why is an exhaustive attack catalog often a weak interview move?
options: It consumes time while hiding whether you can prioritize realistic, high-impact threats.
It proves the candidate is too senior.
It is only a problem in frontend interviews.
It guarantees the system is already secure.
questions.explanation: Interview signal comes from prioritization and architectural consequences.

questions.question: Which threat actor is often missed in system design answers?
options: Buggy or over-privileged internal services and operator workflows.
Only anonymous internet users.
DNS resolvers.
Markdown formatters.
questions.explanation: Internal misuse paths matter, especially in multitenant and admin-heavy systems.

questions.question: What should accompany a security mitigation in a strong answer?
options: A metric, audit trail, or other way to tell whether the control is working.
A promise that incidents will never happen.
A requirement to avoid any trade-offs discussion.
An assumption that logs are unnecessary.
questions.explanation: Controls without observability are difficult to trust or debug.

questions.question: What is the best use of the limited time budget for threat modeling in an interview?
options: Pick the highest-value assets and top attacker paths, then tie them to 2 to 4 concrete mitigations.
Name every OWASP item in alphabetical order.
Skip architecture and focus only on policies.
Discuss encryption without saying what it protects.
questions.explanation: Compact prioritization creates the strongest signal under time pressure.

questions.question: Why is accepted risk worth mentioning?
options: Because senior answers acknowledge that not every mitigation is worth the cost immediately.
Because it avoids all future redesign work.
Because accepted risk does not need monitoring.
Because users prefer undefined security posture.
questions.explanation: Trade-offs and consciously accepted risk are part of realistic design.
